narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 62.5% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10—slightly below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 28, which is well under the average of about 37, and your placement in the lowest decile for school grades in both Year 1 and Year 3, compared to averages around the middle of the pack. Your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, and as a full-time student who's female and Asian, your demographics are not unusual, though Asian applicants make up only about 6% of the pool while whites are over 80%. When the model weighed these factors, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, as being in the bottom group signals potential challenges in sustaining high performance. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Your LSAT score came next in influence, with its lower-than-average result pushing the prediction further toward failure. Family income followed, where your middle bracket didn't provide the boost seen in higher-income profiles, and your racial background as Asian had a modest but notable impact in the same direction. Your undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender rounded out the less dominant factors, all nudging slightly toward the failure prediction without overriding the top issues. Overall, these elements combined to make your profile riskier than most accepted students, who typically show stronger early and later school rankings, higher LSATs, and more balanced academics. To improve your chances next time, focus on actionable steps like retaking the LSAT to aim above 37, building a stronger GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and demonstrating grade improvement in any future decile-measured settings. With targeted prep for the bar itself, like practice exams and study groups, you could shift this prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—you have the determination, and these changes are realistic with effort.
